questions.question: Why are label choices often more dangerous to a metrics platform than raw sample size alone?
options: Because bad labels can explode active-series cardinality and index cost.
Because labels are only stored in browser memory.
Because sample values are usually compressed to zero.
Because label sets automatically replicate across every region.
questions.explanation: Series cardinality is one of the core scaling risks in metrics systems.

questions.question: What is a primary reason to separate hot and cold retention tiers?
options: To keep recent data fast and high-resolution while reducing long-term storage cost.
To prevent dashboards from reading any historical data.
To guarantee every query uses raw one-second samples forever.
To eliminate the need for alert evaluators.
questions.explanation: Retention tiers trade fidelity for cost over time.

questions.question: Which metric best signals a cardinality incident after a bad deploy?
options: Active series count by tenant or metric family.
Median dashboard theme color.
Average office Wi-Fi throughput.
The number of new hire accounts created.
questions.explanation: Active-series growth is the direct symptom.

questions.question: Why should alert evaluation usually have isolated query capacity?
options: Because paging correctness should not depend on whether someone runs a heavy dashboard query.
Because alert evaluators never read recent data.
Because alerts do not use time-series storage.
Because shared query pools reduce durability.
questions.explanation: Operational signal quality needs predictable resources.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of strict ingest-side cardinality budgets?
options: They protect the platform but can block teams unless onboarding and exceptions are well managed.
They remove all need for retention policies.
They guarantee every query is fast forever.
They make downsampling impossible.
questions.explanation: Guardrails help the platform, but they add friction.

questions.question: Which failure mode is most directly indicated by growing alert-evaluator lag?
options: Alert queries are not keeping up with required freshness for paging decisions.
Exporters are no longer adding labels.
All historical retention has been deleted.
Clients stopped viewing dashboards.
questions.explanation: Evaluator lag is an operational risk for alerts.

questions.question: Why is downsampling often acceptable for cold historical queries?
options: Long-range trend analysis usually tolerates lower resolution better than recent dashboards do.
Because downsampling improves label quality automatically.
Because alert evaluators only use one-year data.
Because it guarantees exact per-second replay forever.
questions.explanation: Historical trends usually need less fidelity than hot operational debugging.

questions.question: What is the better first response when one tenant overwhelms shared ingest capacity?
options: Apply per-tenant throttles and cardinality controls rather than let the whole platform degrade equally.
Disable all writes for every tenant globally.
Delete all historical data immediately.
Turn every metric into a log line.
questions.explanation: Isolation is a key multi-tenant design property.
